**Velibor** "**Bora**" **Milutinović** ([Serbian Cyrillic](/wiki/Serbian_Cyrillic_alphabet "Serbian Cyrillic alphabet"): Велибор Бора Милутиновић; born 7 September 1944) is a Serbian former professional [footballer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [manager](/wiki/Manager_(association_football) "Manager (association football)").

He has managed at five editions of the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"), tied for the record alongside Brazilian manager [Carlos Alberto Parreira](/wiki/Carlos_Alberto_Parreira "Carlos Alberto Parreira"), but did so in five consecutive World Cups with different teams: [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico_national_football_team "Mexico national football team") ([1986](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup")), [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica_national_football_team "Costa Rica national football team") ([1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup")), the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") ([1994](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup")), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria_national_football_team "Nigeria national football team") ([1998](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup")) and [China](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") ([2002](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup")), qualifying for 1986 and 1994 as the host nation. He is also the first manager to take four teams beyond the first round – all but China – earning the nickname of *Miracle Worker*,[[1]](#cite_note-1) first given to him by [Alan Rothenberg](/wiki/Alan_Rothenberg "Alan Rothenberg"), then president of the [United States Soccer Federation](/wiki/United_States_Soccer_Federation "United States Soccer Federation").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In total, Milutinović has managed eight [national football teams](/wiki/List_of_men%27s_national_association_football_teams "List of men's national association football teams").

## Managing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Managing career")]

### World Cup national teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: World Cup national teams")]

#### Mexico (1983–86)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mexico (1983–86)")]

Milutinović led Mexico to the quarter-finals at the [1986 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup"), its highest finish. Mexico fell in the quarter-finals to [West Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") on penalty kicks.

#### Costa Rica (1990)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Costa Rica (1990)")]

Milutinović took over [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica_national_football_team "Costa Rica national football team") just before the [1990 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") and got Costa Rica into the second round. In 1990, Milutinović was hired as head coach of Costa Rica just 90 days before the World Cup. He cut the captain and other starters. Costa Rica managed to beat [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") and lost to [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team"), 1–0, before losing 4–1 to [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_national_football_team "Czechoslovakia national football team") in the second round.[[3]](#cite_note-The_Philadelphia_Inquirer-3)

#### United States (1991–95)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: United States (1991–95)")]

[Hank Steinbrecher](/wiki/Hank_Steinbrecher "Hank Steinbrecher"), general secretary of the [U.S. Soccer Federation](/wiki/United_States_Soccer_Federation "United States Soccer Federation"), conducted the job interviews for the [U.S. national team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") head coach position. American coaches had not proved their worth on the international stage, as the United States had lost all three games in the 1990 World Cup finals under [Bob Gansler](/wiki/Bob_Gansler "Bob Gansler"). When the USSF's search began in 1991, the emphasis was not so much on experience, but on finding a coach who could squeeze the last drop of potential out of a lightly regarded team, and Milutinović's name came up again and again.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He had coached first Mexico, then Costa Rica to surprising World Cup success.

Milutinović left no doubts about who ran the team, cutting two U.S. players, [Peter Vermes](/wiki/Peter_Vermes "Peter Vermes") and [Desmond Armstrong](/wiki/Desmond_Armstrong "Desmond Armstrong"), board members of the national federation, from his World Cup team. Milutinović further cut [Bruce Murray](/wiki/Bruce_Murray_(soccer) "Bruce Murray (soccer)"), the all-time leading U.S. goal scorer. When [Alexi Lalas](/wiki/Alexi_Lalas "Alexi Lalas") first showed up at training camp, Milutinović told him to get a haircut or get off the team.[[3]](#cite_note-The_Philadelphia_Inquirer-3)

Milutinović coached the U.S. national team at the [1994 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), hosted in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), where the team notched its first win in the World Cup since 1950 and progressed to the knockout round of the tournament for the first time since the 1930s. This was hailed as a success for a country with little soccer experience. Bora’s name was used for the 1994 World Cup set manufactured by the [Upper Deck Company](/wiki/Upper_Deck_Company "Upper Deck Company").

The USSF fired Milutinović on 14 April 1995, saying it wanted someone who could be both coach and administrator. Milutinović reportedly wanted no part of the administrative duties.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

#### Nigeria (1997–98)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Nigeria (1997–98)")]

Milutinović coached the [Nigerian team](/wiki/Nigeria_national_football_team "Nigeria national football team") at the [1998 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup") in France.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Nigeria won its group, notching a notable 3–2 upset win over [Spain](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_football_team "Spain men's national football team"), and reached the knockout rounds. This was the fourth team that Milutinović had taken to the knockout rounds of the World Cup, a coaching record.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

#### China (2000–02)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: China (2000–02)")]

Under Milutinović's coaching, for the first time ever, the [China national team](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") qualified to be among the 32 finalists for the World Cup in [2002](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"). He was hailed as a hero in China, ending a 44-year drought, and was popularly known as *Milu*.[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, unlike his previous forays, Milutinović could not take the Chinese team past the first round due to their inexperience at the world stage.

### Other national teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other national teams")]

#### Honduras (2003–04)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Honduras (2003–04)")]

In the summer of 2003, Milutinović was in serious negotiations to finally take over the national team at his native [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_national_football_team "Serbia national football team"). Despite heavy, month-long persuasion from Serbian football officials, Milutinović turned down the offer and soon signed on to the [Honduras national team](/wiki/Honduras_national_football_team "Honduras national football team"). He led the team to the first round of [CONCACAF](/wiki/CONCACAF "CONCACAF") qualifiers before resigning on 30 June 2004. He cited "the prevailing bad atmosphere, created by comments made by the country's managers, officials and press" as the reason for his leaving during World Cup qualifying.

#### Jamaica (2006–07)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Jamaica (2006–07)")]

On 16 November 2006, Milutinović was announced as head coach of [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_national_football_team "Jamaica national football team"). On 9 November 2007, following a string of six consecutive friendly defeats, he was fired by the Jamaican FA.

#### Iraq (2009)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Iraq (2009)")]

Milutinović led the [Iraq national football team](/wiki/Iraq_national_football_team "Iraq national football team") in group play in the [2009 FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/2009_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2009 FIFA Confederations Cup") for two draws and one loss, failing to reach the knockout stage.

### Club teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Club teams")]

Milutinović's managerial career at club level has seen more mixed success. His longest managerial spell for a single club was his tenure with [UNAM](/wiki/Pumas_UNAM "Pumas UNAM") of Mexico from 1977 to 1983. Several of his Pumas players ended up playing for Mexico at the 1986 World Cup.

Since then, he has managed briefly for several club teams. He managed [Udinese](/wiki/Udinese_Calcio "Udinese Calcio") of the Italian [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B") for nine matches in 1987. He then managed the [MetroStars](/wiki/MetroStars "MetroStars") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") to the worst record in league history in [1999](/wiki/1999_Major_League_Soccer_season "1999 Major League Soccer season"). He also had a brief stint in the [Qatar Stars League](/wiki/Qatar_Stars_League "Qatar Stars League") with [Al-Sadd](/wiki/Al-Sadd_Sports_Club "Al-Sadd Sports Club") in the [2004–05](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Qatar_Stars_League "2004–05 Qatar Stars League") season.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Milutinovic_Brothers_2.jpg/330px-Milutinovic_Brothers_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milutinovic_Brothers_2.jpg)

The Milutinović brothers (left to right): [Milorad](/wiki/Milorad_Milutinovi%C4%87 "Milorad Milutinović"), [Miloš](/wiki/Milo%C5%A1_Milutinovi%C4%87 "Miloš Milutinović") and Bora

Milutinović comes from a footballing family; he and his two brothers [Miloš](/wiki/Milo%C5%A1_Milutinovi%C4%87 "Miloš Milutinović") and [Milorad](/wiki/Milorad_Milutinovi%C4%87 "Milorad Milutinović") played together for [Partizan](/wiki/FK_Partizan "FK Partizan").

His father was killed in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), his mother by [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") soon after the war. He said he does not remember either of his parents. He was raised by an aunt, and raised playing football.[[10]](#cite_note-soccer-12)

Milutinović is married to a [Mexican](/wiki/Mexicans "Mexicans") and currently resides in [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"). Aside from his native [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"), he is fluent in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language").
